author: @ericheft6184
text: I am new to 3d printing and never looked that deep into resin printing because it seemed like a hassle/messy. I liked the explanation of the smooth resin vs layer lines of the filament printing. It looked like the resin prints all had soft edges.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I rounded them in the design mate to stop a 'foot' forming

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
there are also resins that give you rubbery results, and others that are super tough, dental grade, and all sorts of other things. ;-) 
the ridges are still there because they're formed by layers and smooshing.  there's more smooshing on FDM printers because of how they work.  and the layers are just that much thinner and no smoosh to the resin prints.  technically still layers though and printing a globe will help reveal that on some of the curves, ,and you'll feel them there.  nothing like fdm in size, but still there.  glad you like the results,  I occasionally 3d print 'print in place' fidget spinners with ball bearings in them (on an A1 mini)..  I wonder.. could you compare a bearing made on the resin printer vs an FDM to see how much friction has been removed from the part?    maybe just a spinning wheel and timing it could give a rough percentage difference. :-)

replies.text: an interesting world to explore mate - and yes i can give it a go

Was thinking you could leave holes in the working ends, then plug them with metal cylinders, the same way people install threaded sockets in pla prints. That would increase the rate of heat transfer if it's an issue. Might be a little bit more work, but I can't imagine that the thermal conductivity of the resin is very high. Cheers to you as well, lol. I'm not in a position to get a new printer, but when I am, I'll have to try it out myself

author: @itsumonihon
text: Hi Robert, great video as always. These printers are extremely useful in turning your 3d printed ideas into highly durable metal parts if you add a bit more to your setup. The most cost effective way I can think of for you specifically would be with a microwaveable metal foundry. You should be able to melt 1 or 2 pounds of iron in your microwave without too much trouble using one of those small foundries, and you can pour the molten metal into 3d printed ceramic moulds (and burn out the resin print) or you can make "sandwich" type sand moulds with a top and bottom half where you take the resin piece out before pouring in the metal. If you use proper casting aluminum this is childishly easy to do. Oh yes, and these microwave foundries are cheap - +-$100

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the help mate - i will have a think about it - could well be a way i go - cheers

author: @CosminRotaru
text: I think the wash and cure times are way too long...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fair enough - depends what you want to work with really

replies.author: @CosminRotaru
replies.text: @ i guess depends on the resin. Idk. Usually a few minutes otherwise you'd have problems. In my experience the water washable doesn't seem so water washable. I ended up using IPA. With even shorter wash times: like 3 minutes in the washer and then some extra manual cleaning with a brush in a separate container. When all is done i spray some clear coat on the part to prevent the resin to absorb moisture and protect it for longer uv exposure in the environment. I tell you what - this resin printing is not as easy as I thought 😁

author: @fluffypinkpandas
text:
Your experinentation, and summary of your results is brilliant.
As someone with both a Saturn3Ultra(Resin) and Ender3Pro(Filament), I walk away from this video with newfound insight as to how to best use my tools to achieve the best results for my projects and workflow going forward. My biggest problem with filament was accuracy, but now I know it's strength is more valuable in the right contexts. And my problem with resin was of course the washing - but now I know to just fill up the build plate with the more technical bits, and then its all worthwhile. CHEERS.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - i am really glad it helped

author: @newmonengineering
text: I have had my Saturn 4 since it was released. It has been a great printer. One nice thing you can do is get transparent resin. After you clean the parts off sometimes it gets a little milky white, but just apply a thin layer with a brush of the same rwsin and cure it again and you get nice transparent parts. They are great for demonstration and visualizations. I use the transparent resins with LEDs as well to make certain things light up. Its much more clear than the clear filiment prints with layer lines. I find this to be a great application for resin. And like you said for pistons and valves they work great much closer fit and water tight as well. Sometimes filiment isnt 100% water tight, but the resin is.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very nice points mate - thanks for sharing - i was pondering a fresnel lens with transparent
